bodyText:
Hi Joe,

My team (ABI) has been building upstream data models that I think overlap significantly with some of the data needs you're working through on the sourcing side, and I'd like to make sure we're not duplicating effort.

Specifically, we've established supplier parent/child mappings, category-to-service-line attribution, and clinical & non-clinical volume measurement as part of our data work supporting the Portfolio Expansion project (working with Brian/Jordan/Jennie). I'd love to spend a few minutes understanding what you're pulling together and where there might be a fit.

I’m also trying to figure out the best way to help support what you and Justin’s team are trying to achieve long-term. Attached is what I have for context so far.

I'd also like to tee up a follow-on conversation around some leading‑indicator work we've been doing on contract and program health — early signals on performance trends that could be useful for your analysis down the road.

Looking forward to connecting again!

Matt

bodyText:
Many members (including UnityPoint) are accessing the core Supply Chain tables (Transaction Analysis, Product And Pricing, etc) through the mart but don’t have the skills to effectively pull the data they need. I get asked all the time for help beyond just walking them through the data. This is similar to the ERP data support issues. I think it’s reasonable to ask the Customer Success team to answer questions about the data but there needs to be additional support on how to use it. If we don’t it will ultimately cost us more money as members set up queries and connections to BI tools that consistently pull in too much data beyond what their business use case needs.

 

Steve

bodyText:
Hey Matt,

Quick heads-up on an upcoming expense. We've crafted a statement of work with Pythian to implement more advanced PHI detection and scanning across our Google Cloud environment. Legal (Evan Williams) has reviewed and approved the SOW.

Background: This work is driven by heightened concern (from legal & Todd’s group) over potential liability should any meaningful PHI exist within our cloud platforms where it shouldn’t be. Apparently, we provide much greater indemnity to our customers that the major cloud providers give to us, putting Premier at particular risk should PHI get exposed, even if it's the fault of the cloud provider. While this is primarily an issue with the way we write contracts IMO, it can’t hurt to go an extra mile to ensure that if PHI is making its way into Google (e.g. advisory staff uploading clinical data), we detect it quickly.

Approach: We've leveraged Pythian to help design the solution, but we'll be implementing it ourselves internally to minimize both impact to the team and expense.

Cost & Timeline:

~$70K total expenseTargeting a start in the next month or twoExpected to take a couple of months to completeNo action needed on your end — just wanted to make sure you're aware of the spend and the rationale before it hits. Just let me know if you have any concerns, or if we should delay the work. Currently we plan to begin sometime late next month.

Thanks,
Matt

bodyText:
Hi Crystal,

 

We have finalized a 2‑year Google Cloud Premier Commitment Contract through Pythian, which has been fully negotiated and reviewed by legal and the leadership team.

 

Summary of the agreement

Term: 24 monthsCommitment: $3.1M minimum spendDiscount: 7% on servicesCounterparty: Pythian (direct agreement) 

Why this makes sense

We declined a direct renewal with Google due to significantly higher required commitment levels to maintain prior discounts.By contracting through Pythian, we were able to preserve a comparable discount while keeping the commitment lower and limiting duration to two years.The agreement is expected to generate approximately $217K in savings over the term versus a non‑discounted alternative.From a strategic standpoint, this aligns with our current cloud roadmap: while Databricks remains a long‑term consolidation objective, we have stable, cost‑effective BigQuery workloads that do not warrant near‑term migration. Leadership has confirmed that a 2‑year GCP commitment is appropriate given this posture. 

Status

The approach has been reviewed and approved by Bill, Matt, and Ben, with no outstanding concerns.Pythian has provided a clean, final version and is ready to route via DocuSign upon approval. 

I will route the attached amendment to you for signature via Docusign, please let us know if you have any questions. 

 

Thank you,

Priscilla

bodyText:
Matt, Bill,

Priscilla took the initiative to negotiate directly with Pythian to secure a 2-year recommitment for GCP at a $3.1M minimum spend with a 7% discount.

Why this is a good outcome:
As you’ll recall, we declined to renew directly with Google because sustaining our prior discounts required significantly higher commitment levels. Priscilla managed to secure nearly the same discount through a Pythian-driven commitment, keeping the overall spend on the lower end of our projections and limiting the duration to two years.

Expected financial impact:
If we assume no discounting as the alternative, this agreement should save roughly:
- $108.5K per year (7% of ~$1.55M/year implied by $3.1M over 24 months), or
- $217K over the 2-year term (7% of $3.1M).

Strategic context / why I recommend proceeding:
I recognize the organizational goal to consolidate to Databricks where feasible, and we’ll continue working toward that. However, we have significant BigQuery-based workloads that are stable, perform well, and would be more expensive and disruptive to migrate unnecessarily. For many of these, we can meet the “single plane of glass” objective by surfacing views into Databricks, avoiding a wholesale lift-and-shift of workloads that don’t need to move. Additionally, maintaining a meaningful presence with Google remains strategically useful for negotiation leverage, experimentation, and optionality as Google continues to evolve, especially given their position in the AI landscape.
